stem: A 4-month-old boy is brought to a regional ED for his third admission, this time with 10 days of persistent oral thrush despite two courses of oral nystatin, an erythematous nappy-area candidiasis, and a 5-week history of chronic non-bloody diarrhoea with poor weight gain (current weight on the 3rd centile, having tracked along the 50th at birth). His past admissions in the last 6 weeks have been for pneumocystis-like interstitial pneumonia requiring nasal high-flow and one episode of CMV viraemia. He was born at term to non-consanguineous parents; his mother had a brother who died of an unexplained pneumonia at 7 months of age. He received BCG at birth in his country of birth, and rotavirus oral vaccine at 6 weeks in Australia. On examination he is small and pale, with oral plaques, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on a recent chest X-ray, no palpable lymph nodes or tonsillar tissue, and bilateral fine inspiratory crackles. T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA. FBC today: Hb 96, WCC 5.2 with absolute lymphocyte count 0.6 x10^9/L (well below the age-specific lower limit of around 3.0). Total IgG, IgA, IgM are all low. The paediatric team is concerned about a primary immunodeficiency.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next step?

options.text: Empirical oral fluconazole and clinic review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of a likely SCID; oral fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and the time-critical need for transplant pathway evaluation (ASCIA: SCID).

options.text: Same-day immunology referral, lymphocyte subsets, isolation, no live vaccines
options.rationale: The presentation is highly suspicious for severe combined immunodeficiency: failure to thrive, severe lymphopenia (ALC < 2.5-3.0), opportunistic infection (Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, persistent candidiasis), maternal family history of unexplained infant death, BCG exposure, and absent thymic shadow. Urgent immunology referral, T/B/NK lymphocyte subsets, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines and use of irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical (ASCIA: SCID; eviQ).

options.text: Reassure parents that recurrent infections are normal at 4 months
options.rationale: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age and warrant urgent specialist evaluation (RCH CPG: Recurrent infections; ASCIA).

options.text: Give routine 6-month MMR catch-up vaccination at this visit
options.rationale: Live vaccines including MMR, rotavirus, BCG and varicella are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection; the immediate priority is to stop further live vaccine exposure and confirm the diagnosis (ASCIA: SCID; NCIRS).

options.text: Send HIV antibody (not PCR) and clinic review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists in infants under 18 months irrespective of HIV status; HIV PCR (proviral DNA or RNA) is the test of choice and should be sent in parallel to immunology workup, not as a stand-alone next step (RCH CPG: HIV in children).

stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised child has measles confirmed on PCR after a maculopapular rash spread from the face caudally over 24 hours. He had three days of fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis before the rash, and Koplik spots were identified at the GP visit the day before rash onset. He attended a community playgroup on the day before his rash appeared and the morning of rash onset, while infectious. The public health unit has been notified and is contacting the playgroup register. Among the playgroup contacts identified within the 4 hours before to 4 days after his rash, several need urgent post-exposure management decisions. They include: a 5-month-old infant whose mother is rubella-immune but has no documented measles history or serology; a 13-month-old child who received a single dose of MMR five days ago; a 35-year-old pregnant playgroup helper at 22 weeks gestation with no documented measles immunity and a negative measles IgG on urgent serology done this morning; a 7-year-old child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukaemia in week 2 of induction chemotherapy and currently neutropenic; and a 30-year-old playgroup leader born in 1992 with two documented MMR doses on her workplace immunisation record.
lead_in: Which contact most appropriately receives normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) rather than MMR vaccine as post-exposure prophylaxis?

options.text: The 5-month-old infant, given MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is not given under 6 months of age - it is poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody; infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written (MMR) is wrong for this age (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The 13-month-old who received MMR five days ago, given a second MMR now
options.rationale: MMR five days before exposure is too recent to have generated protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 hours of exposure may still help; NHIG is not first-line here and a single early dose is recorded but the routine schedule (second dose at 18 months) should be followed (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The pregnant 22-week non-immune playgroup helper, given NHIG within 6 days of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is a live vaccine and CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. A non-immune pregnant contact within 6 days of exposure should receive NHIG to attenuate or prevent maternal measles and complications; serology has confirmed she is non-immune (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles; CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: The 7-year-old with ALL on induction chemotherapy, given MMR within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy; this child should receive NHIG, so the option as written is incorrect (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

options.text: The 30-year-old playgroup leader with two documented MMR doses, given a third MMR booster
options.rationale: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention or booster is required for this contact (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

stem: A 6-year-old boy is in week 3 of induction chemotherapy for high-risk B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia at a tertiary paediatric oncology centre. He shared a 1-hour indoor classroom session in the day hospital school room with another child who has since been confirmed by PCR to have early varicella, with a vesicular rash that had been present for less than 24 hours during the session. The exposure was 36 hours ago. The boy has had no past episode of clinical varicella, no varicella vaccination on his record (his older sibling refused MMRV at 18 months for the family on philosophical grounds and the patient was diagnosed before catch-up), and his pre-treatment baseline varicella IgG was negative on serology drawn before induction. He is currently profoundly neutropenic with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.4 x 10^9/L, has no rash, no fever, no oral lesions, and feels well. He has no drug allergies. The parents are anxious and want to know what should happen next, and the oncology team has already isolated him in a single room with contact and airborne precautions while the management decision is finalised. Zoster immunoglobulin and intravenous antivirals are both available on site.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer the varicella vaccine within 72 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and is CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Administer zoster immunoglobulin (ZIG/VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severely immunocompromised susceptible contacts (varicella IgG negative, no prior infection or vaccination) with a significant exposure should receive ZIG within 96 hours (preferably as soon as possible, but the AIH allows extension up to 10 days in some circumstances) to attenuate or prevent disseminated varicella (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Commence prophylactic oral valaciclovir from day 7 to day 21 post-exposure
options.rationale: Antiviral prophylaxis with aciclovir or valaciclovir from days 7 to 14 may be considered as an adjunct or alternative when ZIG is unavailable, but ZIG is the preferred first-line option in this scenario per the AIH (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Reassurance and observation, treating with intravenous aciclovir only if a rash develops
options.rationale: Waiting for the rash to appear before acting forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised children carries significant mortality (RCH CPG: Varicella; Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Send urgent varicella IgG and IgM and decide on prophylaxis based on the result
options.rationale: Repeat serology is unnecessary - the pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative, so he is documented susceptible; waiting for serology delays ZIG and reduces its effectiveness (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

stem: A 32-year-old early childhood teacher who is 18 weeks pregnant attends her GP after multiple confirmed cases of erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) were diagnosed in the kindergarten class she teaches over the last fortnight. She was in close prolonged indoor contact with two affected children one week ago, before they were excluded with their characteristic facial erythema and lacy peripheral rash. She is asymptomatic with no rash, arthralgia, or fever. This is her second pregnancy; her first was uncomplicated and the child is now in her class. Her morphology scan at 18 weeks last week was reported as structurally normal with appropriate growth and amniotic fluid volume, and she is up to date with routine antenatal care, including the first-trimester combined screen which was low risk. She has no past clinical history of slapped cheek and no documented parvovirus B19 serology on file. She has no allergies and is on pregnancy multivitamins only. She has not had any blood transfusion. Examination is normal with no rash, no joint swelling, and an unremarkable obstetric examination with fundal height consistent with dates. The GP is unsure whether she is immune or susceptible, what test to request, and how each possible result will change her antenatal pathway.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Administer normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) intramuscularly within 7 days of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: NHIG is not indicated for parvovirus exposure in pregnancy; there is no proven prophylactic benefit and current Australian guidance recommends serological assessment and surveillance instead (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Arrange urgent amniocentesis for parvovirus B19 PCR to detect vertical foetal transmission
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is invasive and is reserved for confirmed maternal infection with evidence of foetal compromise; it is not a first-line investigation in an asymptomatic exposed contact (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Weekly foetal middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler velocimetry from now for 12 weeks
options.rationale: MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia is appropriate AFTER confirmed maternal infection (typically weekly for 8 to 12 weeks); routinely starting it before serology in an exposed but possibly already-immune woman is inappropriate use of resources (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Maternal parvovirus B19 IgG and IgM serology to determine immune status and acute infection
options.rationale: Serology distinguishes pre-existing immunity (IgG positive, IgM negative - no further action), susceptible non-infected (both negative - repeat in 4 weeks), and recent infection (IgM positive +/- IgG seroconversion - escalate to MFM for serial foetal ultrasound looking for hydrops) (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy; KEMH: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Reassure and discharge - parvovirus B19 has no documented effect on a pregnancy past 12 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal parvovirus B19 infection can cause foetal anaemia and hydrops fetalis with the highest risk in the second trimester (around 9 to 20 weeks); discharge without investigation is unsafe at 18 weeks (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

stem: An 11-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department with a rash and oral pain. He started oral lamotrigine 3 weeks ago after a recent diagnosis of focal epilepsy with secondary generalisation, and the dose was rapidly titrated by his treating neurologist alongside ongoing sodium valproate, which he had been taking for 6 months. He has had no other new medications, no recent herpes simplex outbreak, and no recent Mycoplasma-like respiratory illness. Over the last 36 hours he has developed a flu-like prodrome with fever to 38.7 deg C, malaise, photophobia, and a sore mouth and eyes. Today the rash has spread rapidly. On examination he is unwell, mildly tachycardic, with temperature 38.6 deg C, heart rate 124, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98% room air, and BP 105/65. There are confluent dusky erythematous macules with central duskiness and bullae forming on the trunk and proximal limbs covering an estimated 8% body surface area, with sloughing on light lateral pressure on intact-appearing skin. He has haemorrhagic crusting of the lips, painful ulcers on the buccal mucosa and palate, bilateral red painful eyes with conjunctival injection and mucopurulent discharge, and dysuria with genital erosions visible on inspection. There are some flat lesions on the palms but no classic three-zone targets. He is alert, distressed, and able to drink only sips.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Switch lamotrigine to oral carbamazepine and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome affects younger children (typically <5 years), spares mucous membranes, and is not associated with target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine carries the same SJS risk as lamotrigine and is contraindicated (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Continue lamotrigine at half dose and start oral prednisolone for presumed erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: Erythema multiforme is characterised by classic three-zone target lesions, mostly acral distribution, and is usually post-HSV; the dusky macules with sloughing, positive Nikolsky, recent high-risk drug, and three mucosal sites here are SJS, not EM, and continuing the trigger drug is dangerous (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Commence empirical IV aciclovir and admit to a general paediatric ward for HSV-associated erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; the diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fit SJS and ward-level care is inappropriate intensity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Cease lamotrigine immediately, admit to a paediatric burns/HDU service, and consult ophthalmology and dermatology urgently
options.rationale: Bullae plus positive Nikolsky sign plus involvement of at least two mucosal sites plus recent high-risk drug (lamotrigine, particularly with rapid titration or with concurrent valproate) is Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN spectrum. Immediate cessation of the offending agent, admission to a paediatric burns or HDU equivalent for fluid and skin care, and urgent ophthalmology review (ocular sequelae are the leading long-term morbidity) are mandatory (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome; RCH CPG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol, and arrange neurology review in 2 weeks for lamotrigine dose review
options.rationale: SJS has 5 to 15% mortality with delayed care, and ocular sequelae are sight-threatening - outpatient management is unsafe (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

stem: A 6-year-old previously well fully immunised girl is brought to a tertiary paediatric emergency department by her parents. Five days ago she had varicella that was treated supportively at home, with the rash now beginning to crust over and no new lesions for 48 hours. Three days ago she developed pain in her left thigh which has been progressively worsening - she now refuses to bear weight on that leg and her parents describe the pain as seeming out of proportion to the modest external skin changes they can see. Over the last 12 hours she has become febrile to 39.8 deg C, vomited twice without diarrhoea, and developed a diffuse blanching macular erythematous rash particularly on the trunk and the affected thigh. On arrival she is grey, mottled, and confused, with temperature 39.6 deg C, heart rate 178, respiratory rate 48, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 70/35, and capillary refill 6 seconds in the limbs and 5 seconds centrally. Her left thigh has a 6 x 8 cm area of dusky erythema overlying a healing varicella lesion, with marked tenderness extending well beyond the visible erythema and a small area of skin anaesthesia centrally. There is no obvious crepitus on the limb. Blood cultures have been drawn. Bloods show WCC 24 x 10^9/L with marked left shift, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L (baseline normal), ALT 240 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, and platelets 92 x 10^9/L. The on-call paediatric surgeon and ICU registrar are on their way.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: MRI of the left thigh before intervention to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement
options.rationale: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis - the diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic; resuscitation and antibiotics begin in the resus bay (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Admit, commence IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin for atypical Kawasaki disease
options.rationale: Atypical or incomplete Kawasaki disease can mimic this picture but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, and the dusky soft tissue lesion overlying healing varicella are not Kawasaki; treating as Kawasaki misses life-threatening necrotising fasciitis (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir monotherapy for disseminated varicella with secondary haemodynamic compromise and septic shock
options.rationale: Disseminated varicella in an immunocompetent child is rare; the focal painful thigh lesion with anaesthesia and the systemic toxin-mediated picture point to invasive bacterial superinfection, not viral dissemination (RCH CPG: Varicella).

options.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and admit to a general paediatric ward for scarlet fever observation
options.rationale: Scarlet fever causes a sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and is not associated with shock, lactic acidosis, AKI, or pain disproportionate to skin findings - oral therapy and ward-level care are dangerously inadequate (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

options.text: Resuscitate, give IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin and clindamycin, and call surgery for urgent debridement
options.rationale: Post-varicella, pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, shock and multi-organ derangement (acute kidney injury, transaminitis, lactic acidosis) is invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection plus streptococcal toxic shock syndrome. Broad-spectrum cover (e.g. ceftriaxone) PLUS anti-staphylococcal (vancomycin) PLUS clindamycin (toxin suppression and ribosomal action) are essential, with urgent surgical debridement the definitive intervention - antibiotics do not penetrate necrotic tissue (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).

stem: A 23-year-old woman with established BPD attends the emergency department after taking 8 paracetamol tablets following a fight with her current partner. This is her fourth low-lethality presentation in the past 6 months. Serum paracetamol is below the treatment line at 8 hours and LFTs are normal. On interview she is engaged, has agreed to handover of remaining medication to a relative, identifies her older sister as a strong protective factor able to stay with her tonight, and has a current outpatient DBT clinician who can see her tomorrow morning. She denies current plan or intent for further self-harm, denies psychotic features, and her K-10 is 28. She has no current major depressive episode by SIGECAPS criteria. She has previously had a 4-week voluntary admission that she describes as destabilising, with an increase in self-harm in the week after discharge. Her treating team has a documented care plan recommending brief, focused community management for low-lethality presentations.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with a structured safety plan and next-day DBT review
options.rationale: For low-lethality chronic self-harm in BPD with adequate community supports, NHMRC and RANZCP recommend a structured safety plan (means restriction, identified supports, crisis numbers, early follow-up) and rapid return to the patient's usual psychological therapy; admission is not required when risk can be managed less restrictively (NHMRC BPD; LifeSpan/Black Dog safety planning).

options.text: Brief admission for 48 hours to monitor for further self-harm
options.rationale: Routine short admissions for chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD are not recommended; the NHMRC guideline favours community management with brief admissions only when safety cannot otherwise be managed, and her previous admission worsened her trajectory (NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission criteria
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires mental illness plus risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; she has engaged voluntarily, has a clear safety plan and a treating clinician next day, so the least-restrictive principle is not satisfied (state Mental Health Acts; NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and discharge
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not recommended for the core BPD presentation; starting an antipsychotic at discharge after a low-lethality overdose adds metabolic risk without core benefit (NHMRC BPD).

options.text: Refer for inpatient DBT programme as next-day transfer
options.rationale: Australian DBT is predominantly delivered as an outpatient or day-programme model; inpatient DBT is not standard and her existing outpatient DBT clinician should be the first point of escalation (NHMRC BPD).

stem: An 84-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with a urinary tract infection. On day 3 of intravenous ceftriaxone the nursing staff are concerned that she is withdrawn, eating poorly, sleeping during the day and unable to engage with the ward physiotherapist. Her usual GP letter from 2 weeks ago documents independent ADLs with walking aid, no prior mood disorder, and stable cognition with an MMSE of 27 at her last GP review. On examination she is drowsy but rousable, slow to respond, attention is markedly impaired on backwards-counting, and her thinking is disorganised when she does engage. She has no thoughts of suicide and her affect is flat rather than clearly sad. Sodium is 128 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, WCC 12.6 x10^9/L, CRP 84 mg/L. She is on paracetamol regularly and was started on oxycodone 5 mg PRN for hip pain two days ago. There is no prior antidepressant history.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence sertraline 25 mg daily for late-life depression
options.rationale: This presentation has acute onset, fluctuating attention and disorganised thinking - consistent with hypoactive delirium rather than depression. Starting an antidepressant before treating delirium is premature and risks SIADH worsening hyponatraemia (ACSQHC Delirium CCS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD for behavioural disturbance
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are reserved for delirium with severe agitation or distress that risks harm; hypoactive presentations should not be routinely treated with antipsychotics (ACSQHC; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Withhold oxycodone, correct hyponatraemia and treat the UTI
options.rationale: The CAM features are met for hypoactive delirium. The single most appropriate step is to identify and reverse precipitants: stop the newly added opioid, correct hyponatraemia with judicious fluid management, ensure ongoing UTI treatment, optimise environment, hydration, mobilisation and orientation (ACSQHC Delirium CCS).

options.text: Arrange CT brain to exclude an acute infarct
options.rationale: CT brain is reserved for delirium with focal neurology, head injury, anticoagulation or unexplained drop in conscious level; routine imaging is not first-line in delirium with a clear precipitant (ACSQHC).

options.text: Refer to consultation-liaison psychiatry for ECT consideration
options.rationale: ECT is not first-line in this acute medical context; the diagnosis is not depression and ECT is reserved for severe treatment-resistant or psychotic depression in older adults (RANZCP).

stem: A 79-year-old woman with mild Alzheimer dementia is admitted with a displaced intracapsular fractured neck of femur after a fall at home. The orthopaedic team plan a hemiarthroplasty within 48 hours. She lives independently with a son nearby, manages her own bills, has an advance care directive that does not specifically address fractures, and has no documented enduring guardian. On interview she is alert and conversant, oriented to person and place, knows the year but not the date. She knows she has broken her hip and that the doctors want to do an operation to put it back together. She can repeat back, in her own words, that the operation involves replacing part of the bone, that risks include bleeding, infection and clots, and that without surgery she would be bed-bound with significant pain. She says she wants to have the surgery so she can walk again. Her MoCA today is 22 of 30 with deficits in delayed recall and visuospatial domains.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate determination of capacity for this surgical consent?

options.text: She lacks capacity because her MoCA is below 26
options.rationale: Capacity is not determined by a cognitive test score; the MoCA cut-off is for screening cognitive impairment, not capacity. Decision-specific capacity assessment is required (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP capacity assessment).

options.text: She lacks capacity because of her dementia diagnosis
options.rationale: A diagnosis of dementia does not equal incapacity; many people with mild dementia retain capacity for specific decisions, and assumption of incapacity is contrary to Australian law (Australian Law Reform Commission).

options.text: Defer to her son to provide substitute consent
options.rationale: Substitute decision-makers are engaged only when the person lacks capacity for the decision; defaulting to family bypasses her autonomy (state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: She has capacity for this specific decision at this time
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. She can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the information relevant to this decision in her own words; she meets the functional test for capacity for this surgical consent despite mild dementia (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP; respective state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Request a Mental Health Act assessment for involuntary treatment
options.rationale: The Mental Health Act does not authorise consent to surgical procedures; for incapable patients without an advance directive or guardian, guardianship pathways or emergency consent provisions apply, not the Mental Health Act (state Mental Health Acts).

stem: An 84-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia (premorbid MMSE 22 documented 6 weeks ago) is admitted from her home with a urinary tract infection and dehydration after her daughter found her drowsy. On day 4 of intravenous ceftriaxone the infection has clinically resolved with CRP falling from 156 to 24 mg/L, MSU now sterile, and sodium corrected from 122 to 137 mmol/L. Her attention this morning is back to baseline on backwards-counting, her CAM is negative, her behaviour has settled, and the family-nurse and OT report she is at the cognitive level documented before this admission. She lives alone with twice-weekly family visits and a recently increased home-care package. Today she tells the team she wants to go home and refuses to engage with proposed residential aged care assessment. Her daughter, who is the documented enduring guardian, disagrees and is pushing strongly for nursing home placement. The team is debating whether the patient can refuse the daughter's plan.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Override her refusal because the daughter is her enduring guardian
options.rationale: Enduring guardians make substitute decisions only when the person lacks capacity for the particular decision at the relevant time; if the patient currently has decision-specific capacity, the guardian cannot override her (Australian Law Reform Commission; state Guardianship Acts).

options.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission to keep her in hospital
options.rationale: The Mental Health Act addresses mental illness with risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; it does not authorise placement decisions for cognitive impairment, and there is no acute mental illness requiring detention here (state Mental Health Acts).

options.text: Discharge immediately because the delirium has resolved
options.rationale: Discharge requires assessment of safe destination, supports and (if capacity is in question) a documented capacity assessment; immediate discharge without that step is not in the patient's interest (RACGP; ACSQHC Delirium).

options.text: Continue antibiotics for another 7 days while debate continues
options.rationale: The infection has resolved; ongoing antibiotics expose her to harm without benefit and do not resolve the discharge question (eTG Antibiotic; AMS principles).

options.text: Reassess decision-specific capacity for the discharge destination
options.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. The delirium has now resolved (CAM negative, returned to baseline cognition); a fresh functional assessment for the specific decision of discharge destination is required before any substitute decision-maker is engaged or any application made to the Civil and Administrative Tribunal of the relevant state (ALRC; RACGP; state Guardianship Acts).
